original_hypothesis.title: PEMCertChainLib _findTcb Array Out-of-Bounds When PCESVN Is Not Last SVN Entry
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _findTcb, cpusvns array has size SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE (16) but the loop iterates SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1 (17) times. When the PCESVN_OID is encountered, the value is stored in pcesvn (not cpusvns). If PCESVN_OID is NOT the last entry in the DER sequence, the 17th iteration (i=16) would attempt cpusvns[16], causing an out-of-bounds revert in Solidity 0.8.x. Additionally, if PCESVN appears mid-sequence, cpusvns has a zero-valued gap at that index.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The code assumes PCESVN_OID is always the last SVN component. If Intel changes the PCK certificate format or the SVN ordering changes, all attestation verification would permanently revert. The zero-gap issue could also cause incorrect TCB level matching.

proof_of_signal.pattern: cpusvns = new uint256[](16); loop 0..16 (17 iterations); cpusvns[i] written when i=16 and not PCESVN causes OOB

preconditions: PCK certificate has PCESVN_OID not as the last SVN entry in the DER sequence
Or an extra non-PCESVN SVN component exists at position 16

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Array sized 16 with loop iterating 17 times; out-of-bounds write if PCESVN not last
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: cpusvns[i] = cpusvn where i can be 16 (array size 16, valid indices 0-15)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No bounds check on i before array write; no explicit handling of PCESVN position

analysis_questions: What is the actual ordering of SVN components in Intel PCK certificates?
Is PCESVN always the last component in current Intel SGX certificate implementations?
Does the zero-gap in cpusvns affect TCB level comparison results?
validation_requirements: Parse a real Intel PCK certificate and verify SVN ordering
Test with PCESVN_OID at non-final position to trigger OOB
Verify cpusvns values with PCESVN at different positions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If Intel changes PCK certificate format such that PCESVN is not the last SVN entry, _findTcb would attempt cpusvns[16] (OOB), causing all verifyAttestation calls to revert. This would constitute a permanent DoS of the attestation system.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent DoS of attestation verification if triggered
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All protocol users relying on SGX attestation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract redeployment with fixed parsing

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests for non-standard SVN ordering in PCK certificates

validation_result.mechanism: Array out-of-bounds in _findTcb when PCESVN is not last SVN entry
validation_result.impact_summary: The _findTcb function allocates cpusvns array of size 16 but loops 17 times. If PCESVN_OID is not the last entry, cpusvns[16] causes an OOB revert in Solidity 0.8.x, permanently breaking attestation for non-standard certificate formats. Additionally, when PCESVN appears mid-sequence, cpusvns has a zero-gap corrupting TCB level matching.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _findTcb function in PEMCertChainLib.sol creates an array `cpusvns = new uint256[](SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE)` where SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE is 16, giving valid indices 0-15. The loop iterates `SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1` (17) times with index i ranging from 0 to 16. Inside the loop, when the current OID matches PCESVN_OID, `pcesvn` is set (no array write). Otherwise, `cpusvns[i] = cpusvn` is executed. The code implicitly assumes PCESVN_OID is always the 17th and last entry (at i=16). If PCESVN_OID appears at any earlier position k (0 ≤ k ≤ 15), then: (1) cpusvns[k] remains 0 (zero-gap), (2) subsequent CPU SVN values are written at shifted indices, and (3) when i reaches 16, the else branch executes cpusvns[16] which is out of bounds, causing a Solidity 0.8.x panic revert. This is a genuine code defect - the loop logic conflates the iteration index with the array index without accounting for the PCESVN entry consuming one iteration without an array write.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies lines in PEMCertChainLib.sol containing the _findTcb function. The array allocation at size 16, the loop bound of 17, and the conditional array write at cpusvns[i] are all directly visible in the code. The defect is clear: 17 iterations over a 16-element array with only one iteration (PCESVN match) avoiding the array write means exactly one entry MUST be PCESVN AND it must be the last entry, otherwise OOB occurs.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Array cpusvns has size 16 (indices 0-15). Loop runs 17 times (i=0..16). The PCESVN branch avoids writing to cpusvns[i], but if PCESVN is not at position i=16, the final iteration writes cpusvns[16] which is out-of-bounds, causing a revert. Even when PCESVN appears mid-sequence, cpusvns[k] at the PCESVN position remains zero, creating incorrect SVN values for TCB comparison.

calling_sequence: _findTcb: allocates cpusvns[16], loops 17 times, writes cpusvns[i] for non-PCESVN entries
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When decodeCert is called with isPckCert=true, it calls _findPckTcbInfo which calls _findTcb. In _findTcb, cpusvns is allocated with size SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE (16). The loop iterates SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE + 1 (17) times. For each iteration, if the OID matches PCESVN_OID, pcesvn is set. Otherwise, cpusvns[i] is written. If PCESVN_OID is not the last (17th) SVN entry in the DER-encoded certificate, then on iteration i=16, the else branch executes cpusvns[16], which is an out-of-bounds access. Solidity 0.8.x automatically checks array bounds and reverts with a Panic(0x32) error.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that configure PCK certificate format. The deployment script DeployOnL1.s.sol deploys PEMCertChainLib as a standalone contract: `PEMCertChainLib pemCertChainLib = new PEMCertChainLib();`

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: In the current Intel SGX PCK certificate specification, PCESVN is indeed the last (17th) SVN component in the TCB extension. However, the code does not validate this ordering assumption. If Intel changes the PCK certificate format or SVN ordering in the future, or if a crafted certificate with non-standard ordering is provided (though it would need to pass signature verification), the _findTcb function would revert with an array OOB panic, causing a permanent DoS of all attestation verification that encounters such certificates. The zero-gap issue is also a real defect: if PCESVN appears at position k < 16, cpusvns[k] stays 0 and all subsequent CPU SVN values are at shifted indices, leading to incorrect TCB level matching in _checkTcbLevels via _isCpuSvnHigherOrGreater.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The defect is real but currently mitigated by Intel's consistent certificate format placing PCESVN last. If the format changes or an unexpected certificate is encountered: (1) OOB revert causes DoS for attestation verification, (2) zero-gap in cpusvns causes incorrect TCB level comparisons potentially accepting or rejecting attestations incorrectly. Since the contract is non-upgradeable, a format change would require redeployment.
recommended_mitigations: Use a separate counter for cpusvns array index that only increments when a non-PCESVN entry is processed: `uint256 cpuIdx = 0; ... if (isPCESVN) { pcesvn = svnValue; } else { cpusvns[cpuIdx++] = svnValue; }`
Add a bounds check: `require(i < SGX_TCB_CPUSVN_SIZE, 'cpusvn index overflow')` before writing to cpusvns[i]
Validate that exactly 16 CPU SVN entries and exactly 1 PCESVN entry are found after the loop completes
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Construct a DER-encoded PCK certificate extension where PCESVN_OID appears as the first SVN entry (position 0) instead of the last. 2. Call decodeCert with isPckCert=true on this crafted certificate. 3. Observe that _findTcb reverts with Panic(0x32) when i=16 attempts cpusvns[16]. 4. Alternatively, test with PCESVN at position 8 to observe both the zero-gap in cpusvns and the OOB revert.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers in the source may vary from the hypothesis if the file has different formatting than expected, but the logic is clear from the code
Whether Intel will ever change the SVN ordering in PCK certificates is unknown, but the code should be robust regardless
The crafted certificate would still need to pass signature verification in _verifyCertChain to cause real-world impact, though the parsing defect exists independently
additional_testing_needed: Unit test with crafted DER data where PCESVN_OID appears at non-final position to confirm OOB revert
Unit test with PCESVN at various positions (0, 8, 15) to verify zero-gap behavior in cpusvns
Integration test to verify that the current Intel PCK format places PCESVN last, confirming current operational safety
